entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WHETHER MARRIAGE SHOULD BE REJECTED.
entries.body: Stirred up by the depraved minds of the false apostles, who in their hypocrisy were teaching that marriage ought to be rejected in order that they might appear to be holier than others, the Corinthians wrote to Paul to ask him about these things. Because they were unhappy about this teaching, they ignored everything else and concentrated exclusively on this. {fn:0}

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: PAUL’S REPLY.
entries.body: This is Paul’s reply to those who had written to him about this subject. He forbade fornication because it was against the law, but he allowed marriage as being holy and an antidote to fornication. However, he praised chastity as more perfect still. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.body: The Corinthians were asking Paul whether it was right for lawfully married Christians, once they were baptized, to enjoy sexual relations with each other. Paul answered by praising chastity, condemning fornication and allowing conjugal relations. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 200

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: DO NOT DESTROY YOUR SPOUSE’S HAPPINESS IN YOUR QUEST FOR HOLINESS.
entries.body: You have given up your wife, to whom you are bound. This is a big step you have taken. You are not abusing her, you say, but claiming that you can be chaste and live more purely. But look how your poor wife is being destroyed as a result, because she is unable to endure your purity! You should sleep with your wife, not for your sake but for hers. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: THE COMMAND TO HAVE SEX IS NOT ABSOLUTE.
entries.body: People who want to be promiscuous argue that God commanded us to have sexual relations, so that the earth would be filled with human beings. But God is quite capable of making humans out of the earth, as he did at the beginning, so this is no excuse. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 7

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: ENCOURAGING VIRGINITY.
entries.body: Some people think that this was written primarily for priests, but judging from what follows, this cannot be right. If he had meant it only for priests, he would have said so, but throughout this [chapter] he speaks of persons in general. Paul permits marriage as a concession, but the very fact that it is designed to avoid fornication shows that he is really trying to encourage virginity. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.1

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: HUSBANDS AND WIVES SUBMIT TO ONE ANOTHER SEXUALLY.
entries.body: Husband and wife must submit to one another in this matter, since the two of them are one flesh and one will, according to the law of nature. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: HUSBANDS AND WIVES CALLED EQUALLY TO SEXUAL ACCOUNTABILITY.
entries.body: Human laws demand that women be chaste and if they are not they are punished for it, but they do not demand the same from men. Since it was men who made the laws, they did not make themselves equal with woman but allowed themselves extra indulgence. The holy apostle, however, inspired by divine grace, was the first one who made the law of chastity apply to men as well. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 201

footnotes.text: PG 82:271. This selection shows that among the ancient Christian writers there existed an awareness of the social location of men effecting legislative bias, which Christian ethics seeks to correct.

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: THE WIFE DOES NOT RULE OVER HER OWN BODY, NOR THE HUSBAND HIS.
entries.body: If this is so, a man should be able to refrain from exercising his authority [to receive his spouse’s body]. And isn’t a woman capable of doing likewise? {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: EQUALITY IN CHASTITY.
entries.body: The wife does not have power over her own body but is both the servant and the mistress of the husband. If you reject this, you have offended God. But if you want to withdraw yourself sexually, it should only be with your husband’s permission, and then only for a short time. In other parts of Scripture the husband is given certain prerogatives in marriage, but not here. Where chastity is concerned, husband and wife have equal rights. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.1

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: THE HOLY MYSTERY OF MARRIAGE.
entries.body: The mysteries of marriage ought to be performed with holiness, deliberately and without disorderly passions. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE HAZARD OF REFUSING THE MARRIAGE BED.
entries.body: Great evils spring from this sort of continence, if it is overdone. Adulteries, fornications and the destruction of families have often resulted from this. If a married man commits fornication, how much more will he do so if his wife denies herself to him? Unless there is mutual consent, continence in this case is really a form of theft. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.3

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: DO NOT REFUSE ONE ANOTHER.
entries.body: According to this, if he had wished to practice continence but you had not, he would have been obliged to give in to you, and God would have given him credit for continence for not refusing intercourse out of consideration for your weakness, not his own, in order to prevent you from committing adultery. How much better would it have been for you, for whom subjection was more appropriate, to yield to his will in rendering him the debt, since God would have taken account of your intention to observe continence, which you gave up in order to save your husband from destruction. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: DEVOTION REKINDLED BY OCCASIONAL ABSTINENCE.
entries.body: It is not arduous and difficult for faithful married people to do for a few days what holy widows have undertaken and which holy virgins do throughout their lives. So let devotion be kindled and self-gratification be checked. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: WITHHOLDING MAY ELICIT ANGER.
entries.body: If a woman stays away from her husband, she will make him angry, and vice versa. That is why Paul insists that it must be by mutual consent. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 201

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: A PERSONAL CHOICE.
entries.body: No one should be forced to do something unlawful on the ground that he is forbidden to do what is lawful. It is up to each person to discern which path to follow. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: MARRIAGE AMONG BELIEVERS.
entries.body: Marriage is a spiritual gift, but not if it is contracted with unbelievers. The Spirit of God is not given to dwell in those who are not believers. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: PAUL AS EXAMPLE.
entries.body: Paul often puts himself forward as an example when he is talking about difficult subjects. Here is another case in point. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.3

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: AVOID FORNICATION.
entries.body: Paul’s intention is to avoid fornication, not to put hindrances in the way of those seeking a higher way of life. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: MARRIAGE A GIFT OF GOD.
entries.body: Paul comforts those who are married by saying that marriage is a gift of God. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 202

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.body: And even he who withholds himself from his wife often makes her an adulteress when he does not satisfy her desires, even though he does so under the appearance of greater gravity and self-control. And perhaps this man is more culpable who, so far as it rests with him, makes her an adulteress when he does not satisfy her desires than he who, for other reason than fornication, has sent her away—for poisoning or murder or any of the most grievous sins. But as a woman is an adulteress, even though she seems to be married to a man, while the former husband is still living so also the man who seems to marry her who has been put away, does not so much marry her as commit adultery with her according to the declaration of our Savior.

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE STRONG PULL OF CONCUPISCENCE.
entries.body: Paul states that continence is better, but he does not attempt to pressure those who cannot attain to it. He recognizes how strong the pull of concupiscence is and says that if it leads to a lot of violence and burning desire, then it is better to put an end to that, rather than be corrupted by immorality. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.3

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: WHETHER WIDOWS ARE SAID TO BE UNMARRIED.
entries.body: We must not understand these words to mean that widows are not unmarried because they once embraced the married state. Widows are unmarried, but not all unmarried are widows. That is why Paul makes a distinction here. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Clement of Alexandria
entries.title: AFLAME WITH PASSION.
entries.body: Such a person [who cannot exercise self-control] is not sinning against the covenant [by marrying], but neither is he fulfilling the highest purpose of the gospel ethic. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE REMEDY FOR LUST.
entries.body: Why do you acknowledge that there is a necessary remedy for lust yet contradict me when I say that lust is a disease? If you recognize the remedy, then recognize the disease as well. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: INSTRUCTIONS FROM THE LORD.
entries.body: Paul’s views on this subject do not come from himself but from God, who is speaking through him. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.4

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: WHETHER ONE CAN LEAVE ONE’S SPOUSE.
entries.body: Paul did not intend this to apply to those who abandon their spouses for the service of Christ. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: THE WORDS OF THE LORD.
entries.body: Here Paul recalls the words of the Lord [in Matthew 5:32]: Whoever puts away his wife, except for fornication, makes her an adulteress. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle of Paul to the Corinthians 204

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE HUSBAND SHOULD NOT DIVORCE HIS WIFE.
entries.body: A woman may not marry if she has left her husband because of fornication or apostasy, if he wishes to have sexual relations with her. But if the husband turns away from the faith or desires to have extramarital sexual relations, the wife may neither marry another nor return to him. The husband should not divorce his wife, but one should add the clause except for fornication. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: AVOID SEPARATIONS.
entries.body: Separations are best avoided if at all possible, but if not, the wife should not take another husband. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.4

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: NEITHER SPOUSE MAY DIVORCE.
entries.body: We are here given to understand that neither spouse may divorce the other if both are believers. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WHEN A HUSBAND IS AN UNBELIEVER.
entries.body: Paul says this in the case of two Gentiles, one of whom has become a believer. Normally, a pagan detests Christianity, and a Christian does not want to be contaminated by paganism, which is why Paul says that if they are happy to stay together, they should continue to do so. {fn:0}

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: STAYING WITH AN UNBELIEVER.
entries.body: Paul did not mean that a woman should marry an unbeliever, only that she should stay with him if she is already married. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: THE BELIEVING SPOUSE SANCTIFIES THE UNBELIEVING SPOUSE.
entries.body: Husband and wife are one in the same way that wine and water are one when they are mixed together. Just as the believing partner sanctifies the unbelieving one, so the unbelieving partner corrupts the believing one. This is why a man who is not yet married should consider very carefully and either not marry at all or marry only in the Lord. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: PROTECTION FROM DETESTING CHRIST.
entries.body: These unbelievers have the benefit of good will, which protects them from detesting the name of Christ. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN A HARLOT AND A SPOUSE.
entries.body: If a man joined to a harlot is one body with her, then it is clear that a woman joined to an idolater is one body with him. That is true, but in this case she does not become unclean as a result. On the contrary, her cleanness overcomes the uncleanness of her husband, just as the cleanness of a believing husband overcomes the uncleanness of his unbelieving wife. How can this be, if a husband is not condemned for casting out a wife who has played the harlot? The reason is that in this case there is hope that the lost member may be saved through the marriage, whereas in the other case, the marriage has already been dissolved through harlotry. An unbelieving man may be reclaimed by his believing wife if she is faithful to him. But for a harlot things are not so easy, because how can she reclaim someone whom she has wronged? The same is true for a believing husband who has an unbelieving wife. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.4

entries.author: Tertullian
entries.title: YOUR CHILDREN DESTINED FOR HOLINESS.
entries.body: The children of believers were in some sense destined for holiness and salvation, and in the pledge of this hope Paul supported those marriages which he wished to continue. {fn:0}

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: THE FAITH OF THE PARENT OVERCOMES UNBELIEF.
entries.body: When the children are clean and holy, uncorrupted by unbelief, the faith of the parent has won. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: IF THE UNBELIEVING PARTNER DESIRES TO SEPARATE.
entries.body: A marriage contracted without devotion to God is not binding, and for that reason it is not a sin if it is abandoned because of God. But the unbelieving partner sins both against God and against the marriage, because he or she is unwilling to live in a marriage relationship dedicated to God. It is not right to go to court over this because the one who leaves the marriage is doing so out of hatred for God, and for this reason he or she is not to be considered worthy of such attention. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: ANNULMENT IS BETTER THAN SACRIFICING TO IDOLS.
entries.body: If an unbeliever wants his partner to join him in sacrificing to idols, it is better for the marriage to be annulled, so that there may be no breach in godliness. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.4

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: REFUSAL TO REMAIN MARRIED.
entries.body: A Christian husband may leave his wife without any blame, even if they are lawfully married, if she refuses to live with him because he is a Christian. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: LOYALTY TO GOD FIRST.
entries.body: The Lord must come before a husband or a wife. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 7

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: THE BELIEVER NOT TO BE THE CAUSE OF DIVORCE.
entries.body: The believing partner is not to be the cause of the divorce. But if the unbelieving partner wants to separate, the believing partner is innocent and free from any accusation. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 205

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE UNBELIEVER MAY BELIEVE.
entries.body: Paul says this because it is always possible that the unbelieving partner will come to believe if he or she does not detest the name of Christ. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: KEEPING TO GOD’S ASSIGNMENT.
entries.body: God has assigned to each person the time of his salvation, that is, the time when he might believe, and he will keep that person until then. Paul tells the Corinthians that this is his general rule, so that when they hear that others are expected to follow it, they will be more willing to do so themselves. It is always easier to do something when you see others doing the same. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodore of Mopsuestia
entries.title: CIRCUMSTANCES DO NOT IMPEDE HOLINESS.
entries.body: It is wrong to suppose that the circumstances which prevailed when a person was converted stand in the way of his becoming holy. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: DO NOT REMOVE THE MARKS OF CIRCUMCISION.
entries.body: Someone who has been circumcised should not think that this is going too far, or regret it, because in its own day it was necessary. But neither should he seek to convert the uncircumcised to that practice. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 7

entries.author: Theodore of Mopsuestia
entries.title: LET HIM NOT SEEK CIRCUMCISION.
entries.body: It is not possible to replace flesh which has been cut off in this way, although the blessed Epiphanius of Cyprus says that it is.{fn:0} Those who want to know more about the subject can consult what he has to say about it. {fn:1}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

footnotes.text: In De mensuris et ponderibus (On Weights and Measures) 16.

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: NO REWARD FOR CIRCUMCISION OR UNCIRCUMCISION.
entries.body: Circumcision means nothing by itself, but it was a command of God. On the other hand, uncircumcised is the way God made us, so there is no reward for that either. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: MARRIED OR SINGLE.
entries.body: In itself, the state in which we are called is a matter of indifference. An unmarried man, for example, can live a life of purity, but he may also be deeply involved in sin. The same is true for a married man as well. If it were only Christians who practiced celibacy, then it would be possible to say that it was a pure and divine state. But the Marcionites also practice it, though not in the same way as Christians. Christians do it in order to please God, but the Marcionites do it in order not to succumb to the Creator. Celibacy is honorable only when it is clothed with the life and behavior of the church, with pure knowledge and truth. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: STATUS HIGH OR LOW.
entries.body: God does not care about our social status but about our will and mind. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 7

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.body: Paul says this because it makes no difference to God either way. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: OUR SLAVERY AND OUR FREEDOM.
entries.body: I am a slave of that affair and care to which I am bound. For I know that it is written that whatever one is conquered by, to that he is delivered as a slave. . . . Who will free me from this most unseemly slavery except him who said, If the Son shall make you free, you shall be free indeed{fn:0}? {fn:1}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: A SLAVE WHEN CALLED.
entries.body: Paul encourages slaves to serve their earthy masters well, so that they may appear to them to deserve their freedom. A slave who did not do his work properly would blaspheme the name of Christ and do nothing to further God’s cause. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: DO NOT USE RELIGION AS AN EXCUSE.
entries.body: Paul is saying that no slave should run away, using religion as his excuse. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 207

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: CALLED IN THE LORD.
entries.body: Whoever has been delivered from sin is truly free. The ancients used to say that anyone who acts unwisely is a slave. They called all wise men free, and all the unwise were slaves as far as they were concerned. In any case, even a free believer is a slave of Christ, for to be free from God is the greatest sin of all. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: FREEDOM FROM REAL SLAVERY.
entries.body: A slave is free because he has been set free from the passions and diseases of the mind. Legal freedom is unimportant by comparison. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.5

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: SLAVES OF CHRIST AND FREE IN CHRIST.
entries.body: Paul wants to show that slave and master are equal. We are all freedmen of Christ because he has set us free from the tyranny of Satan, and we are voluntary slaves of Christ because, having set us free, he led us into his own kingdom. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: BOUGHT BACK.
entries.body: Christ came and bought us back when we were serving that lord to whom we sold ourselves by sinning. So he appears to have recovered as his own those whom he created. He has redeemed a people who chose to belong to another, who sought another lord for themselves by sinning. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Basil the Great
entries.title: THE DEMONIC PLOT TO REVERSE REDEMPTION.
entries.body: The price of man is the blood of Christ. You have been bought, it is said, with a price; do not become the slaves of men. The powers of the evil one are trying to render this price useless to us. They try to lead us back into slavery even after we are free. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: SO HIGH A PRICE.
entries.body: We have been bought at so high a price that only Christ, who owns everything, is able to pay it. Therefore whoever is bought with a price ought to serve all the more, in an effort to pay back the buyer. Having been bought by God, we must not once again become slaves of men. Slaves of men are those who accept human superstitions. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: DO NOT BECOME SLAVES OF MEN.
entries.body: Free men can enslave themselves by agreeing to serve others out of gluttony, greed or a desire for power. Such a person may be technically free, but in reality he is more of a slave than anyone else. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.5

entries.author: St. Jerome
entries.title: NO GREATER PRICE.
entries.body: What greater price is there than that the Creator shed his blood for the creature? {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Basil the Great
entries.title: IN WHATEVER STATE CALLED.
entries.body: The apostle himself practiced very meticulously what he preached to others. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE COMMAND REPEATED.
entries.body: Paul repeats what he said above in order to underline its importance. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: THE OPTIONAL DISTINGUISHED FROM THE OBLIGATORY.
entries.body: Some rules are given as commandments of God, while others are more flexible and left by God to the decision of the individual. The first kind are those commandments which pertain to salvation. The others are better, because even if we do not keep them, we shall still be saved. There is no merit in doing what is obligatory, but there is in doing that which is optional. {fn:0}

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: RECOMMENDING WITHOUT IMPOSING.
entries.body: It is clear that Paul says this not because he has no command to teach about virginity but because God has not told him that these people should practice chastity themselves. That is why he writes to them giving his opinion and recommending chastity, without imposing it on them. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: AN OPINION, NOT A COMMAND.
entries.body: Paul is coming here to a higher wisdom, but he is hesitant to impose it directly because that might show up the inadequacy of his hearers. Therefore he sets it out as an opinion rather than as a command. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: VIRGINITY BETTER.
entries.body: Here Paul teaches that virginity is better, not just because it is more pleasing to God but also because it is the more sensible course to follow in the present [end-time] circumstances. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: DO NOT SEEK MARRIAGE IF YOU ARE FREE.
entries.body: Paul says that no one should be divorced from his wife except in a case of fornication. As for the unmarried, what advantage is there in giving in to the lusts of the flesh? {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: SPARING THEM WORLDLY TROUBLES.
entries.body: The virgin is spared earthly troubles and set free by her purity, as she awaits the blessed Bridegroom. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: IF YOU MARRY, YOU DO NOT SIN.
entries.body: The man who marries does not sin because he is doing something which is permitted. But if he refuses to do it, he earns merit and a crown in heaven, for it takes great self-control to avoid doing something which is not expressly forbidden. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THOSE CALLED TO VIRGINITY SHOULD NOT MARRY.
entries.body: Paul is not speaking about the woman who has chosen virginity, for if such a woman decides to marry, she has indeed sinned. If widows are condemned for entering into second marriages,{fn:0} how much more is this true of those called to remain virgins. {fn:1}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.7

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE TIME HAS GROWN SHORT.
entries.body: Paul means by this that the end of the world is coming soon. Given this fact, believers should not worry about having children and instead should dedicate themselves to the service of God. For there will be many unprecedented pressures on them, and many will fall into the devil’s trap. No one among us who has a proper fear of the pressures which the Savior predicted will want to be caught like that. {fn:0}

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: MARRIAGE IN THE END TIME.
entries.body: If married people are supposed to live as if they were single, how is it possible not to prefer virginity? {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. Caesarius of Arles
entries.title: BEGET SPIRITUAL CHILDREN.
entries.body: Those who practice physical sterility should observe fruitfulness in souls, and those who cannot have earthly children should try to beget spiritual ones. All our deeds are children. If we perform good works every day, we shall not lack spiritual offspring. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THOSE WHO MOURN LIVE AS THOUGH NOT MOURNING.
entries.body: Those who know that the end of the world is near realize that they will soon be consoled, and they comfort each other with this hope. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE FORM OF THIS WORLD IS PASSING AWAY.
entries.body: Note that Paul says that the form of this world is passing away, not the substance of it. Therefore if the form of the world is going to perish, there is no doubt that everything in the world will vanish. It will all pass away. Every day the world gets older. {fn:0}

entries.author: Apostolic Constitutions
entries.body: A virgin is not ordained, for we have no such command from the Lord [1 Cor 7:25]; for this is a state of voluntary trial, not for the reproach of marriage, but on account of leisure for piety.

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: READINESS FOR WORSHIP.
entries.body: Here Paul explains why virginity is preferable to marriage. It has nothing to do with the rightness or wrongness of sex. Rather it is a question of anxieties which prevent the mind from concentrating on the worship of God. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: FREEDOM FROM ANXIETY.
entries.body: To be concerned about the things of the Lord is not anxiety but salvation. Paul has just told them that he wants them to be free of anxiety. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. Basil the Great
entries.title: THE CONCERNS OF GOD AND MARRIAGE.
entries.body: Paul allows marriage and considers it worthy of blessing, but he contrasts it with his own preoccupation with the concerns of God and hints that the two things are incompatible. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: MARRIAGE AND TEMPTATION.
entries.body: Looking after a wife and family is a worldly thing. Sometimes, just to keep them happy, it even leads to doing things which ought to be punished. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: THE WALL OF VIRGINITY.
entries.body: The unmarried woman has her wall of virginity, protecting her against the storms of this world. Thus fortified by the enclosure of God’s protection, she is disquieted by no winds of this world. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE HUMAN SPIRIT TENDS TO SANCTIFY OR CORRUPT THE BODY.
entries.body: It is the human spirit which either sanctifies or corrupts the body. If anyone tries to have a pure body but a corrupt soul, he will soon have to choose between them. Either the soul must be honored, or the body will be drawn toward corruption. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE SAME CALL TO HOLINESS IN A MARRIED WOMAN.
entries.body: This should not be interpreted to mean that a married woman is not meant to be holy in body as well as in soul. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: TO PROMOTE GOOD ORDER, NOT TO RESTRAIN.
entries.body: What Paul has just said may seem harsh to some people, which is why he adds this here. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: FREE CHOICE OF INDIVIDUALS.
entries.body: Paul makes his case for celibacy, but in the end he leaves it up to the free choice of the individual. If after all this he were to resort to compulsion, it would look as if he did not really believe his own statements. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.7

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: BETTER TO MARRY PUBLICLY THAN BEHAVE BADLY IN PRIVATE.
entries.body: Paul always wants the best out of Christians. If someone really wants to get married, then it is better to marry publicly according to the permission given than to behave badly and be ashamed in private. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: BEING UNDER NO NECESSITY.
entries.body: The evil is not in the cohabitation but in the impediment to purity in the life. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 19.7

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: BE FIRMLY ESTABLISHED IN THE HEART.
entries.body: It is clear from this that someone who has been overcome by apparent impropriety, even if married, is not yet firmly established in the work of the Lord. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: DOING WELL, DOING BETTER.
entries.body: The one is bound by marriage bonds, the other is free. One is under the law, the other under grace. Marriage is good because through it the means of human continuity are found. But virginity is better, because through it are attained the inheritance of a heavenly kingdom and a continuity of heavenly rewards. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: DELIVERED FROM WORLDLY CARES.
entries.body: The one who refrains from marriage does better because he earns merit for her with God and delivers her from the cares of this world. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: A WIFE IS BOUND TO HER HUSBAND.
entries.body: Paul writes this in order to make it clear that a woman who has been rejected by her husband is not free to marry again. If he should die, then she may remarry, but only in the Lord, which means without any suspicion of wrongdoing and within the bounds of the church. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: SEEK RECONCILIATION.
entries.body: The death referred to here is clearly the death of the body, not of the soul. Forgiveness and attempts at reconciliation of the offending person are offered as better solutions than divorce. {fn:0}

entries.author: Shepherd of Hermas
entries.title: REMARRIAGE NOT A SIN.
entries.body: If a wife or a husband falls asleep and the one left marries again, is that a sin? No, but if he remains single he covers himself with greater honor and glory in the sight of the Lord. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: HAPPY, HAPPIER AND HAPPIEST.
entries.body: Happy is the woman who has a husband. Happier still is the widow who can remain chaste with little effort on her part. But happiest of all is the virgin, who can attain to the highest prize without struggling. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 7

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: ENTIRE DEVOTION TO GOD IS HAPPIER.
entries.body: What Paul means is that she is blessed if she marries and has a husband to protect her, but she is more blessed if, for the sake of piety, she refuses marriage and devotes herself entirely to God. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: DEGREES OF HAPPINESS.
entries.body: Note that Paul does not say that a woman who contracts a second marriage will be unhappy. Rather he says that she will be happier if she remains single. It is all a matter of degree. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 212

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE SPIRIT ENABLES GOOD COUNSEL.
entries.body: Paul adds that he has the Spirit of God in order to show that his advice is reliable. {fn:0}
